edHandoff.summary: 68-year-old female with severe COPD (FEV1 35% predicted) presenting with 3 days worsening dyspnea and increased sputum production, now purulent. On 3L NC in ED, SpO2 88% on arrival improved to 92% on 2L. Given duonebs x3, methylprednisolone 125mg IV. ABG shows pH 7.32, pCO2 58, pO2 68 on 2L. CXR with hyperinflation but no consolidation. Not currently on BiPAP but tiring.
edHandoff.disposition: Admit to medicine/stepdown for acute hypercapnic respiratory failure. May need BiPAP. Avoid intubation if possible.

sources.name: GOLD Report 2024 - Management of COPD Exacerbations

variant.diagnosis: Severe COPD Exacerbation with Acute Hypercapnic Respiratory Failure

patient.communicationStyle: Speaks in short sentences due to dyspnea. Knows her disease well. Afraid of intubation — she's been told she may not come off the ventilator. Uses pursed-lip breathing.

chiefComplaint.onset: Worsening over 3 days. Started with increased sputum, then dyspnea worsened progressively.
chiefComplaint.severity: Worst it's been since last hospitalization 8 months ago

present: increased sputum production (from 1 tablespoon to half a cup daily)
purulent sputum (changed from white to yellow-green 2 days ago)
worsening dyspnea at rest (baseline: dyspnea walking 1 block → now: dyspnea at rest)

pmh: Severe COPD (GOLD Stage III, FEV1 35% predicted on last PFTs 6 months ago)
2 exacerbations requiring hospitalization in past year

socialHistory.livingSituation: Lives with husband who is her primary caregiver. Home oxygen concentrator.
socialHistory.advanceDirectives: Has healthcare proxy (husband). Full code but has expressed she would NOT want prolonged mechanical ventilation if prognosis is poor.

familyHistory: Father: COPD, died at 72 from respiratory failure

physicalExam.general: Elderly woman sitting upright, tripod position, using accessory muscles, speaking in 3-4 word sentences. Pursed-lip breathing. Appears fatigued.
physicalExam.heent: Pursed lips. No cyanosis of lips currently.
physicalExam.neck: Accessory muscle use (SCM, scalenes). No JVD.
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular. Distant heart sounds. No murmurs.
physicalExam.pulmonary: Barrel chest. Diffuse expiratory wheezes bilaterally. Prolonged expiratory phase (I:E ratio approximately 1:4). Decreased breath sounds at bases. No crackles. Using accessory muscles.
physicalExam.abdominal: Soft, non-tender. Liver edge palpable 2cm below costal margin (hyperinflation, not hepatomegaly).
physicalExam.extremities: No edema. Clubbing absent. Warm and well-perfused.
physicalExam.neurologic: Alert but fatigued. Oriented x4. Mild asterixis noted (CO2 narcosis sign).

labs.note: Acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis. Elevated bicarb suggests chronic CO2 retention with acute decompensation. Expected bicarb for chronic: ~32. Acute component: pH should be ~7.36 for chronic pCO2 58 → actual 7.32 = acute-on-chronic.
Mild polycythemia from chronic hypoxemia. WBC mildly elevated.

labs.value: Na 138, K 4.0, Cl 96, HCO3 30, Cr 0.8, Glucose 128
labs.note: Elevated bicarb = renal compensation for chronic respiratory acidosis
Low — suggests viral or non-infectious trigger more likely than bacterial pneumonia

imaging.findings: Hyperinflated lungs with flattened diaphragms. Increased AP diameter. No focal consolidation, effusion, or pneumothorax. Heart size normal. No pulmonary edema.
imaging.impression: Severe COPD without acute pneumonia or heart failure.

admittingOrders.oxygenTarget: SpO2 88-92% (AVOID over-oxygenation — risk of worsening hypercapnia via Haldane effect and V/Q mismatch)
admittingOrders.niv: Start BiPAP: IPAP 12, EPAP 5, titrate for comfort and ABG improvement. Goal: reduce work of breathing, improve pH >7.35, reduce pCO2. Keep patient on BiPAP as much as tolerated (target >4 hours initially, ideally 8+ hours including overnight).
admittingOrders.bronchodilators: Albuterol/ipratropium (Duoneb) nebulized q4h scheduled + q2h PRN. Switch to MDI with spacer when improving.
admittingOrders.steroids: Methylprednisolone 40mg IV q12h x 48 hours, then prednisone 40mg PO daily — total course 5 days. (REDUCE study: 5-day course as effective as 14-day)
admittingOrders.antibiotics: Azithromycin 500mg IV day 1, then 250mg IV daily x 4 days (Anthonisen criteria met: increased dyspnea + increased sputum volume + purulent sputum = all 3 cardinal symptoms). Avoid erythromycin per allergy.
admittingOrders.monitoring: ABG in 2 hours after BiPAP initiation. Vitals q2h. Continuous pulse oximetry. Respiratory therapy q4h. Intubation supplies at bedside.
admittingOrders.other: Elevate HOB 30-45°. Continue home tiotropium. Hold fluticasone/salmeterol while on nebulized therapy. Resume on discharge. Speech therapy consult if aspiration concern. Social work for home services assessment. Advance directives discussion given severity — document goals of care.

teaching.correctDiagnosis: Severe COPD Exacerbation with Acute Hypercapnic Respiratory Failure
keyLearningPoints: Acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis: elevated HCO3 tells you this is chronic CO2 retainer with acute worsening
BiPAP reduces intubation rates by ~60% in acute COPD exacerbations — use it early
Target SpO2 88-92% — over-oxygenation can worsen hypercapnia (Haldane effect + blunted hypoxic drive)
Anthonisen criteria for antibiotics: increased dyspnea, increased sputum volume, purulent sputum (2 of 3 for moderate exacerbation, all 3 for severe)
Short steroid course (5 days) is as effective as longer courses (REDUCE trial)
This patient has advance directives preferences — always address goals of care early in severe COPD
mustNotMiss: Pneumonia (CXR clear, but recheck if not improving)
Pneumothorax (sudden worsening + unilateral decreased breath sounds)
Pulmonary embolism (COPD exacerbation that doesn't improve with standard therapy)
Heart failure exacerbation (BNP is normal here, but often coexists)
commonPitfalls: Over-oxygenating to SpO2 100% — this can suppress respiratory drive and worsen CO2 retention
Not starting BiPAP early enough — waiting for further deterioration increases intubation risk
Prolonged steroid courses — 5 days is the evidence-based duration
Forgetting to continue long-acting inhalers during hospitalization
Not having the goals-of-care conversation — this patient has expressed preferences about mechanical ventilation
teaching.attendingQuestions: Walk me through the ABG. Is this purely acute, purely chronic, or acute-on-chronic? How do you know? And what's your oxygen target — and why?
